content.text: Cross-Team Coordination on Release Trains

A release train for a GenAI application touches more teams than a conventional service release. Platform owns the CI pipeline and Kubernetes cluster. The ML team owns prompt files and model version pins. The application team owns business logic and retrieval. The safety team owns red-team evaluation and must sign off before production. The data team owns index updates that affect retrieval quality.

Without explicit coordination structure, each team operates on its own timeline and the train becomes a negotiation rather than a process. The release manager's job is to make dependencies visible, establish a shared calendar, and assign decision authority clearly — not to gate everything through a single person.

attrs.alt: Cross-team release train coordination model showing teams feeding into the train, dependency map, comms cadence, and RACI matrix

content.text: The Release Manager Role
The release manager (RM) is a coordination role, not an authority role. The RM does not decide what ships — that is the scope of the team leads and VP Engineering. What the RM owns:
Maintains the train scope document.
Gathers planned PRs and eval targets from each team at the sprint planning meeting. Keeps the document current as the window progresses.
Surfaces cross-team dependencies.
Identifies when an application PR depends on a platform change or an ML PR depends on a data index update. Flags these at the mid-sprint risk review so teams can unblock each other before the freeze.
Runs the freeze and deploy meetings.
Cuts the release branch at freeze time, runs the scope check CI job, and chairs the eval review and deploy approval meetings.
Communicates train status.
Posts status updates to the
#releases
Slack channel at each phase transition. Product managers and support leads read this channel and do not need to attend meetings to know what is shipping.
Dependency Mapping Before Freeze

Cross-team dependencies are the most common cause of last-minute deferrals. A dependency map built at the start of the sprint lets teams see what they are blocked on and resolve it during the development window rather than at the freeze meeting:

Run this script at the Wednesday W1 dependency check and again at the Monday W2 risk review. Any unresolved dependency at Monday W2 is at high risk of causing a deferral — the team with the blocking PR gets a direct Slack message with a deadline.
Safety Team as a First-Class Train Participant
The safety team is not a reviewer that gets pinged at the last minute — they are a scheduled participant at two specific points in the train cycle:
Sprint planning:

The safety reviewer reads the proposed scope and flags any PRs that require extended safety evaluation — for example, a new prompt category or a change to the content moderation logic. These get additional red-team cases added to the eval suite before the development window closes.
Eval review (Fri W2):

The safety reviewer reads the full safety eval report, reviews any new failure modes found during staging soak, and provides the safety sign-off required to open the production approval gate. This sign-off is separate from the eval accuracy sign-off and cannot be delegated.

## Safety Reviewer Checklist (Eval Review Meeting)

**Train:** 2025-W28  |  **Safety reviewer:** @jsmith

### Scope review
- [ ] Read all PRs in scope that touch prompts, model version, or retrieval
- [ ] Confirm safety eval suite covers the change surface (no new categories uncovered)
- [ ] No unresolved red-team cases from this train's staging soak

### Eval results
- [ ] Safety pass rate: **1.0** (any failure = automatic veto)
- [ ] Jailbreak resistance: **pass** on all injected test cases
- [ ] Refusal accuracy: no new unintended refusals (false positive rate stable)
- [ ] Toxicity filter: no regressions in staging soak output sample

### Sign-off
- [ ] **APPROVED** — safety evaluation passed; no blocking concerns
- [ ] **VETO** — safety evaluation failed; reason: ___________

Signature: @jsmith  |  Timestamp: 2025-07-18T15:22:00Z
Automating Cross-Team Status Visibility
A bot that posts to
#releases
at each CI event means team leads do not need to attend meetings to know the current train state. The release channel becomes the single source of truth:
Handling Conflicting Team Timelines

The most common coordination failure is when two teams have a dependency but different readiness timelines. The platform team is ready to merge an infrastructure change, but the application team's PR that depends on it won't be ready for three more days. Strategies for handling this:
Merge the dependency first; defer the dependent.

The platform change merges to this train. The application PR that depends on it targets the next train, where it has a clean foundation. This is the default — it prevents the platform team from being held up by the application team's schedule.
Hold both for the next train.
If the platform change is risky enough that it shouldn't ship without the application change that exercises it, both wait. Risk without coverage is worse than delay.
Use a feature flag to decouple.

The platform change ships but the application behavior behind a feature flag stays disabled until the next train enables it. This requires the feature flag infrastructure to be reliable and observable.
ML Team Coordination: Prompt and Model Freeze Protocol

The ML team has a tighter internal freeze deadline than the rest of the train — prompts freeze on Wednesday W1, a full week before the code freeze. This gives the staging environment time to accumulate real traffic against the new prompt before the freeze meeting reviews results. The ML team's internal process:

## ML Team Train Checklist (per train)

### Sprint Planning (Mon W1)
- [ ] List all prompt changes targeting this train (file + PR)
- [ ] List all model version pins targeting this train
- [ ] Declare eval delta for each change in train scope document
- [ ] Confirm eval dataset covers new prompt categories (if any)
- [ ] Flag any changes that need extended red-team coverage to Safety team

### Prompt Freeze (Wed W1 — 1 week before code freeze)
- [ ] All prompt PRs merged to main before 17:00 UTC
- [ ] Prompt digest recorded in train scope document
- [ ] Staging environment redeployed with new prompts
- [ ] Start 7-day soak: monitor accuracy and safety on staging traffic

### Mid-Sprint Review (Mon W2)
- [ ] Review staging soak metrics: any surprises?
- [ ] Any new edge cases to add to eval fixture set?
- [ ] Confirm model version pinned in config/model_versions.json

### Code Freeze (Wed W2)
- [ ] No further prompt changes unless cherry-pick exception approved
- [ ] Eval run triggered on release branch
- [ ] Eval review attendance confirmed for Fri W2

### Eval Review (Fri W2)
- [ ] Present accuracy delta vs declared target per PR
- [ ] Flag any regressions beyond declared targets
- [ ] Provide recommendation: approve / defer / investigate

The 7-day prompt soak between the ML team's internal freeze and the code freeze is the key adaptation for GenAI trains. Seven days of staging traffic catches failure modes that unit tests and fixture-based evals miss — low-frequency query patterns, retrieval edge cases, and subtle tone regressions that only emerge at scale.
Data Team Coordination: Index Snapshots and Schema Changes

The data team's contributions to a train are often indirect: a corpus update that changes what the retrieval system returns, or an index schema change that requires an application migration. Both affect eval results and must be coordinated explicitly.
Corpus updates (non-breaking):

New documents added to the retrieval index without schema changes. These can ship in any train, but the data team must notify the ML team if the update changes coverage for any prompt category so the eval suite can be checked.
Schema changes (breaking):

A change to the index schema (new fields, renamed fields, changed embedding model) requires a coordinated migration: data team merges the new schema, application team updates the retrieval client, both must land in the same train or the application breaks.
Staging snapshot refresh:

The staging retrieval index is a weekly snapshot of the production corpus. If the data team ships a large corpus change, the staging snapshot must be refreshed before the eval run so staging results reflect production data distributions.

# .github/workflows/refresh-staging-index.yml
# Triggered by data team when a corpus update ships to prod
name: Refresh Staging Index Snapshot

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      reason:
        description: "Reason for refresh (e.g., train 2025-W28 corpus update)"
        required: true

jobs:
  refresh:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Sync prod corpus to staging bucket
        run: |
          aws s3 sync s3://prod-corpus/ s3://staging-corpus/ \
            --delete \
            --only-show-errors

      - name: Rebuild staging retrieval index
        run: |
          kubectl -n staging create job --from=cronjob/index-builder \
            staging-index-refresh-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M)

      - name: Wait for index build completion
        run: |
          kubectl -n staging wait --for=condition=complete job \
            --selector=app=index-builder --timeout=30m

      - name: Notify release manager
        run: |
          curl -X POST ${{ secrets.SLACK_RELEASES_WEBHOOK }} \
            -H 'Content-type: application/json' \
            -d '{"text": ":arrows_counterclockwise: Staging index refreshed for ${{ github.event.inputs.reason }}"}'
Escalation Paths When Coordination Breaks Down

Even with good process, coordination failures happen. A team misses the dependency check, a PR lands in the wrong state at freeze, or the safety reviewer is unavailable for the eval review. Each failure mode has a defined escalation path so the train does not get blocked indefinitely:
Failure Mode
Immediate Action
Escalation if Unresolved in 4h
Dependency unresolved at Monday W2
RM pings both teams directly, sets 48h deadline
Defer dependent PR; unblock the dependency separately
Safety reviewer unavailable for eval review
Backup reviewer from safety team covers
Delay eval review by 24h; push deploy window by 1 day
Eval shows surprise regression not in declared targets
ML lead investigates root cause within 24h
Defer offending PR; ship rest of train with updated scope
Release manager unavailable during freeze week
Pre-designated backup RM (on rotation)
VP Eng fills RM role for that train only

Escalation paths are defined in a runbook, not improvised. When a failure occurs, the team should be able to look up the exact steps without waiting for the RM to decide what to do. This is especially important for the safety reviewer absence case — a train that cannot get a safety sign-off must not deploy, and the team needs to know the backup path without escalating to VP Eng for permission.
Summary
Release trains in GenAI systems span platform, ML, application, safety, and data teams — each with their own scope and timeline that must be coordinated against a shared freeze.
The release manager is a coordination role: surfacing dependencies, maintaining the scope document, and chairing phase-transition meetings — not a single-point gatekeeper for decisions.
Cross-team dependencies are mapped at sprint planning and checked again mid-sprint; any unresolved dependency by Monday W2 is at high risk of deferral and gets explicit follow-up.
The safety team attends sprint planning and the eval review — not as a last-minute reviewer but as a participant whose sign-off is a required gate to production.
A status bot posts to a read-only releases channel at each CI phase event, giving all teams current train state without requiring them to attend meetings.
